Here is the full report:

---

## 1. Next.js Version

**Next.js 16.2.0-canary.41**

This is confirmed in both:
- `/home/user/workspace/package.json` -- `"next": "16.2.0-canary.41"`
- `/home/user/workspace/node_modules/next/package.json` -- `"version": "16.2.0-canary.41"`

This is a canary (pre-release) build of Next.js 16.2. The project uses React 19.1.0 and react-dom 19.1.0 alongside it.

---

## 2. Does `node_modules/next/dist/docs/` Exist?

**Yes, it exists and contains 380 files** across a full documentation tree with 4 top-level sections:

| Directory | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `01-app/` | App Router docs (getting started, guides, API reference, glossary) |
| `02-pages/` | Pages Router docs (getting started, guides, building your app, API reference) |
| `03-architecture/` | Architecture docs (accessibility, fast refresh, compiler, supported browsers) |
| `04-community/` | Community docs (contribution guide, rspack) |

Plus a top-level `index.mdx`.

Key sub-structure of `01-app/03-api-reference/` includes:
- `03-file-conventions/` -- `default.mdx`, `page.mdx`, `error.mdx`, `forbidden.mdx`, `dynamic-routes.mdx`, `intercepting-routes.mdx`, `instrumentation.mdx`, `instrumentation-client.mdx`
- `06-cli/` -- `next.mdx`, `create-next-app.mdx`
- `07-edge.mdx`, `08-turbopack.mdx`

Key sub-structure of `02-pages/04-api-reference/` includes:
- `01-components/` -- `image.mdx`, `link.mdx`, `head.mdx`, `script.mdx`, `font.mdx`, `form.mdx`
- `02-file-conventions/` -- `proxy.mdx`, `instrumentation.mdx`, `src-folder.mdx`, `public-folder.mdx`
- `03-functions/` -- `get-static-props.mdx`, `get-server-side-props.mdx`, `get-static-paths.mdx`, `use-router.mdx`, etc.
- `04-config/01-next-config-js/` -- ~38 config option docs
- `05-cli/`

All files are `.mdx` format.

---

## 3. Project Structure

The workspace at `/home/user/workspace` is minimal:

```
/home/user/workspace/
  .gitignore
  AGENTS.md
  CLAUDE.md  ->  symlink to AGENTS.md
  app/
    layout.tsx
    page.tsx
  next.config.ts
  node_modules/
  package.json
  package-lock.json
  tsconfig.json
```

- **`app/` directory**: EXISTS (App Router project)
- **`pages/` directory**: DOES NOT exist
- **`src/` directory**: DOES NOT exist
- **No other top-level directories** besides `app/` and `node_modules/`

The project is a TypeScript project (`tsconfig.json`, `next.config.ts`).

---

## 4. Existing Route Files

There are exactly **2 route files**, both in the `app/` directory using the App Router pattern:

### `/home/user/workspace/app/layout.tsx` (Root Layout)
- Exports metadata (title: "Random Number", description)
- Standard root layout wrapping children in `<html>` and `<body>`

### `/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx` (Home Page at `/`)
- A simple homepage component rendering "Random Number Generator" heading
- Contains a placeholder paragraph: "Please implement a random number that changes on each request."
- No dynamic/server logic yet -- it is a static component

These two files together form a single route: the root path `/`.
</output>

attributes.io.output:
1 --- 2 title: page.js 3 description: API reference for the page.js file. 4 --- 5 6 The `page` file allows you to define UI that is **unique** to a route. You can create a page by default exporting a component from the file: 7 8 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 9 export default function Page({ 10 params, 11 searchParams, 12 }: { 13 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 14 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 15 }) { 16 return

My Page

17 } 18 ``` 19 20 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 21 export default function Page({ params, searchParams }) { 22 return

My Page

23 } 24 ``` 25 26 ## Good to know 27 28 - The `.js`, `.jsx`, or `.tsx` file extensions can be used for `page`. 29 - A `page` is always the **leaf** of the route subtree. 30 - A `page` file is required to make a route segment **publicly accessible**. 31 - Pages are [Server Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components) by default, but can be set to a [Client Component](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-client). 32 33 ## Reference 34 35 ### Props 36 37 #### `params` (optional) 38 39 A promise that resolves to an object containing the [dynamic route parameters](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) from the root segment down to that page. 40 41 ```tsx filename="app/shop/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 42 export default async function Page({ 43 params, 44 }: { 45 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 46 }) { 47 const { slug } = await params 48 } 49 ``` 50 51 ```jsx filename="app/shop/[slug]/page.js" switcher 52 export default async function Page({ params }) { 53 const { slug } = await params 54 } 55 ``` 56 57 | Example Route | URL | `params` | 58 | ------------------------------------ | ----------- | --------------------------------------- | 59 | `app/shop/[slug]/page.js` | `/shop/1` | `Promise<{ slug: '1' }>` | 60 | `app/shop/[category]/[item]/page.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ category: '1', item: '2' }>` | 61 | `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ slug: ['1', '2'] }>` | 62 63 - Since the `params` prop is a promise, you must use `async/await` or React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to access the values. 64 - In version 14 and earlier, `params` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future. 65 66 #### `searchParams` (optional) 67 68 A promise that resolves to an object containing the [search parameters](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Learn/Common_questions/What_is_a_URL#parameters) of the current URL. For example: 69 70 ```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 71 export default async function Page({ 72 searchParams, 73 }: { 74 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 75 }) { 76 const filters = (await searchParams).filters 77 } 78 ``` 79 80 ```jsx filename="app/shop/page.js" switcher 81 export default async function Page({ searchParams }) { 82 const filters = (await searchParams).filters 83 } 84 ``` 85 86 Client Component **pages** can also access `searchParams` using React’s [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) hook: 87 88 ```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 89 'use client' 90 import { use } from 'react' 91 92 export default function Page({ 93 searchParams, 94 }: { 95 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 96 }) { 97 const filters = use(searchParams).filters 98 } 99 ``` 100

1 --- 2 title: page.js 3 description: API reference for the page.js file. 4 --- 5 6 The `page` file allows you to define UI that is **unique** to a route. You can create a page by default exporting a component from the file: 7 8 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 9 export default function Page({ 10 params, 11 searchParams, 12 }: { 13 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 14 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 15 }) { 16 return

My Page

17 } 18 ``` 19 20 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 21 export default function Page({ params, searchParams }) { 22 return

My Page

23 } 24 ``` 25 26 ## Good to know 27 28 - The `.js`, `.jsx`, or `.tsx` file extensions can be used for `page`. 29 - A `page` is always the **leaf** of the route subtree. 30 - A `page` file is required to make a route segment **publicly accessible**. 31 - Pages are [Server Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components) by default, but can be set to a [Client Component](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-client). 32 33 ## Reference 34 35 ### Props 36 37 #### `params` (optional) 38 39 A promise that resolves to an object containing the [dynamic route parameters](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) from the root segment down to that page. 40 41 ```tsx filename="app/shop/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 42 export default async function Page({ 43 params, 44 }: { 45 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 46 }) { 47 const { slug } = await params 48 } 49 ``` 50 51 ```jsx filename="app/shop/[slug]/page.js" switcher 52 export default async function Page({ params }) { 53 const { slug } = await params 54 } 55 ``` 56 57 | Example Route | URL | `params` | 58 | ------------------------------------ | ----------- | --------------------------------------- | 59 | `app/shop/[slug]/page.js` | `/shop/1` | `Promise<{ slug: '1' }>` | 60 | `app/shop/[category]/[item]/page.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ category: '1', item: '2' }>` | 61 | `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ slug: ['1', '2'] }>` | 62 63 - Since the `params` prop is a promise, you must use `async/await` or React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to access the values. 64 - In version 14 and earlier, `params` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future. 65 66 #### `searchParams` (optional) 67 68 A promise that resolves to an object containing the [search parameters](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Learn/Common_questions/What_is_a_URL#parameters) of the current URL. For example: 69 70 ```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 71 export default async function Page({ 72 searchParams, 73 }: { 74 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 75 }) { 76 const filters = (await searchParams).filters 77 } 78 ``` 79 80 ```jsx filename="app/shop/page.js" switcher 81 export default async function Page({ searchParams }) { 82 const filters = (await searchParams).filters 83 } 84 ``` 85 86 Client Component **pages** can also access `searchParams` using React’s [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) hook: 87 88 ```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 89 'use client' 90 import { use } from 'react' 91 92 export default function Page({ 93 searchParams, 94 }: { 95 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 96 }) { 97 const filters = use(searchParams).filters 98 } 99 ``` 100

attributes.io.output: 1 --- 2 title: connection 3 description: API Reference for the connection function. 4 --- 5 6 The `connection()` function allows you to indicate rendering should wait for an incoming user request before continuing. 7 8 It's useful when a component doesn't use [Dynamic APIs](/docs/app/guides/caching#dynamic-rendering), but you want it to be dynamically rendered at runtime and not statically rendered at build time. This usually occurs when you access external information that you intentionally want to change the result of a render, such as `Math.random()` or `new Date()`. 9 10 ```ts filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 11 import { connection } from 'next/server' 12 13 export default async function Page() { 14 await connection() 15 // Everything below will be excluded from prerendering 16 const rand = Math.random() 17 return {rand} 18 } 19 ``` 20 21 ```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher 22 import { connection } from 'next/server' 23 24 export default async function Page() { 25 await connection() 26 // Everything below will be excluded from prerendering 27 const rand = Math.random() 28 return {rand} 29 } 30 ``` 31 32 ## Reference 33 34 ### Type 35 36 ```jsx 37 function connection(): Promise 38 ``` 39 40 ### Parameters 41 42 - The function does not accept any parameters. 43 44 ### Returns 45 46 - The function returns a `void` Promise. It is not meant to be consumed. 47 48 ## Good to know 49 50 - `connection` replaces [`unstable_noStore`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/unstable_noStore) to better align with the future of Next.js. 51 - The function is only necessary when dynamic rendering is required and common Dynamic APIs are not used. 52 53 ### Version History 54 55 | Version | Changes | 56 | ------------ | ------------------------ | 57 | `v15.0.0` | `connection` stabilized. | 58 | `v15.0.0-RC` | `connection` introduced. | 59

1 --- 2 title: connection 3 description: API Reference for the connection function. 4 --- 5 6 The `connection()` function allows you to indicate rendering should wait for an incoming user request before continuing. 7 8 It's useful when a component doesn't use [Dynamic APIs](/docs/app/guides/caching#dynamic-rendering), but you want it to be dynamically rendered at runtime and not statically rendered at build time. This usually occurs when you access external information that you intentionally want to change the result of a render, such as `Math.random()` or `new Date()`. 9 10 ```ts filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 11 import { connection } from 'next/server' 12 13 export default async function Page() { 14 await connection() 15 // Everything below will be excluded from prerendering 16 const rand = Math.random() 17 return {rand} 18 } 19 ``` 20 21 ```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher 22 import { connection } from 'next/server' 23 24 export default async function Page() { 25 await connection() 26 // Everything below will be excluded from prerendering 27 const rand = Math.random() 28 return {rand} 29 } 30 ``` 31 32 ## Reference 33 34 ### Type 35 36 ```jsx 37 function connection(): Promise 38 ``` 39 40 ### Parameters 41 42 - The function does not accept any parameters. 43 44 ### Returns 45 46 - The function returns a `void` Promise. It is not meant to be consumed. 47 48 ## Good to know 49 50 - `connection` replaces [`unstable_noStore`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/unstable_noStore) to better align with the future of Next.js. 51 - The function is only necessary when dynamic rendering is required and common Dynamic APIs are not used. 52 53 ### Version History 54 55 | Version | Changes | 56 | ------------ | ------------------------ | 57 | `v15.0.0` | `connection` stabilized. | 58 | `v15.0.0-RC` | `connection` introduced. | 59
